hypothesis_package.headline: Assessment scores predict promotion better under strong managers
hypothesis_package.summary: The hypothesis says HiPo assessment scores will better forecast 12-month promotion velocity when employees report to highly effective managers, and will be less accurate under weaker managers. In low-effectiveness settings, the same scores may overstate who is truly ready for advancement. For executives, that means manager quality can determine whether talent assessments are genuinely calibrated…
hypothesis_package.hypothesis_statement: Assessment scores will be more predictive of 12-month promotion velocity for HiPo employees who report to managers in the top quartile of a pre-registered manager-effectiveness index than for otherwise similar HiPos reporting to bottom-quartile managers; in low-effectiveness-manager settings, the same scores will be systematically over-calibrated relative to realized advancement.
hypothesis_package.mechanism_explanation: The specific causal path is that manager effectiveness determines whether psychometric promise is translated into promotable evidence. High-effectiveness managers convert latent potential into visible advancement by assigning stretch work, giving frequent corrective feedback, sponsoring employees into calibration discussions, and advocating for readiness at promotion checkpoints. Low-effectiveness managers do the opposite: they narrow exposure, delay feedback, and suppress sponsorship, so assessed potential does not accumulate the work signals that promotion committees use. As a result, the assessment model is better calibrated when manager behavior creates the intermediate evidence needed for advancement, and it overstates readiness when that conversion process is weak. This makes manager quality a validation control on the assessment system, not just a general employee-outcome moderator.

dimension_notes.business_value: This could materially change executive choices about whether to invest in manager capability versus rely on assessment-based talent pipelines, and it is strategically important, actionable, and likely to yield useful insight—though its value is capped somewhat by modest outcome data and the risk that the effect is not fully separable from other confounders.
dimension_notes.strategic_importance: This is strategically important because it directly affects the organization’s core talent-pipeline question—whether assessment-based HiPo signals can reliably predict leadership success once manager quality is accounted for—and the answer would shape both succession decisions and manager-development investment.
dimension_notes.actionability: If confirmed, this points directly to two concrete levers—manager-quality intervention and better targeting of HiPo/assessment signals by context—making it highly actionable, though not quite maximally so because the org would still need to identify which manager behaviors to change and how to operationalize them.
dimension_notes.organizational_impact: If true, this would affect a large share of the talent pipeline by changing how the organization interprets assessment signals, develops managers, and allocates promotion/succession decisions, but it is still concentrated on high-potential populations rather than the whole business.
dimension_notes.feasibility: 2/9 target constructs map onto known data categories.
dimension_notes.novelty: The idea is more sophisticated than a standard manager-quality versus performance comparison because it frames managers as moderators of how psychometric potential translates into outcomes, but the underlying theme is still a well-trodden HR question about leadership effect and talent development.
dimension_notes.depth: This hypothesis goes beyond a surface correlation by positing a leader-mediated conversion mechanism—manager quality changes whether psychometric potential translates into realized outcomes—but it is still a fairly standard moderation/enablement story rather than a deeply novel causal model.
dimension_notes.expected_insight: If true, this would be meaningfully informative because it links psychometric potential to realized outcomes through manager quality, but it is also a fairly plausible and well-known moderation effect in talent systems, so the likely insight is solid rather than truly surprising.
dimension_notes.confidence: The mechanism is highly plausible and consistent with known manager effects on development and retention, but confidence is only moderate because it depends on separating true manager-quality effects from selection, team-context, and measurement bias with limited longitudinal validation data.
dimension_notes.future_extensibility: If supported, this would open a highly productive program of follow-on questions about manager quality as a moderator of talent signals, enabling segmentation by role/family, coaching interventions, and refinement of promotion/attrition models across contexts.

critique.executive_relevance: Moderate, but diluted. Executives may care about manager quality as a talent-multiplier, but this version is framed as an analytical observation rather than a decision point. It does not clearly tell leaders what to do, where to intervene, or how much value is at stake.
issues: The core idea is not novel: manager quality affecting employee outcomes is a standard, almost default HR hypothesis. The only interesting part is the interaction with psychometric promise, but that is underdeveloped.

It is too broad and too many outcomes are bundled together (performance, promotion velocity, attrition). That makes it look like a generic talent-management claim rather than a sharp testable hypothesis.

The mechanism is plausible but still high-level. ‘Manager quality amplifies or suppresses return on assessment signals’ is intuitive, not a hidden mechanism. It needs a more specific causal path and measurable manager behaviors.

It risks confounding selection effects: higher-potential employees may be assigned to better managers, or better managers may be placed in stronger business units. Without a credible identification strategy, any observed effect will be hard to interpret.

The hypothesis does not specify what ‘lower-quality managers’ means operationally, which matters because the construct could be past performance, engagement scores, coaching behavior, attrition rates, or something else entirely.

It is not clear whether the executive decision is about manager development, talent placement, succession calibration, or assessment-model weighting. A hypothesis should support one primary decision, not four.

Compared with the prior hypotheses, this is very similar in structure: it is another moderator-style claim about when talent signals convert into downstream outcomes, so it is unlikely to be viewed as additive insight rather than repetition.
The business implication is stated, but not quantified. Executives need to know whether the effect size is large enough to justify changing manager development or placement policy.

There is a risk of tautology: if managers are part of the system that determines performance and promotion, saying manager quality affects those outcomes can become self-fulfilling unless the analysis isolates incremental effects beyond prior performance and team context.

The hypothesis is framed in a way that a downstream agent could test only if it has clean manager-quality measures, stable employee-manager links, and longitudinal outcome data. The current data description suggests those inputs may be thin or noisy.
suggested_improvements: Narrow the hypothesis to one primary outcome, ideally promotion velocity or attrition, and one time window.
Define manager quality using a concrete, pre-registered metric: e.g., 360 feedback, team engagement delta, calibrated manager effectiveness score, or subordinate promotion/retention residuals.
Specify the interaction more sharply: for example, ‘assessment score predicts promotion only under top-quartile managers, not bottom-quartile managers.’

State the executive decision explicitly: should the org target manager development, prioritize HiPo placement under stronger managers, or down-weight psychometrics in succession planning when manager quality is low?

Add a causal identification plan or at least a comparison strategy that reduces confounding, such as same-function/business-unit comparisons, manager fixed effects, or quasi-random manager assignment where available.
Replace the generic ‘amplifies or suppresses’ language with a measurable mediator, such as feedback frequency, stretch assignment allocation, or sponsorship visibility.

If the goal is strategic, elevate the claim from ‘manager quality matters’ to ‘manager quality changes the validity of the assessment model,’ because that is the more consequential executive question.

Consider reframing around calibration error: ‘Assessment scores overpredict success in low-quality-manager environments and are better calibrated in high-quality-manager environments.’ That is more diagnostic and testable.
If the organization wants a real decision, ask whether to invest in manager capability as a governance control on pipeline prediction, not merely as a culture initiative.

grounded.rationale: The clearest direct proxy is the precomputed High-Potential composites, with the OPQ domain scores providing the underlying psychometric assessment inputs. Together these operationalize assessed potential / HiPo score used in the hypothesis.

These enterprise leadership domain composites are the best available manager-level proxy for overall manager quality / effectiveness and can be used to define top- vs bottom-quartile manager-effectiveness groups.
The enterprise leadership domain scores are the most direct available operationalization of leadership effectiveness; the hypothesis explicitly frames this as the manager-effectiveness index.

Manager-perspective 360 ratings are the closest available performance-related evaluation measure. No dedicated performance appraisal column is listed, so these serve as the best proxy for rated performance.
tenure_years is the only available HR outcome that partially proxies retention by reflecting how long an employee remains employed.

Report-perspective 360 ratings are the closest available proxy for the employee’s experience of manager behavior such as coaching and feedback quality, though they are indirect and not a dedicated coaching measure.

target_population.description: High-potential employees with completed assessment data and an identified direct manager, observed within an organization that has manager-effectiveness and 360-style rating data available.
inclusion_criteria: Employees with at least one HiPo assessment score recorded in the grounded columns
Employees with a corresponding manager-effectiveness score available for the relevant manager
Employees with at least one manager-related 360 rating available
Employees who are current employees at the time of assessment, so manager-linked outcome signals can be observed
exclusion_criteria: Employees without an identified direct manager in the available data
Employees missing both HiPo assessment scores and manager-effectiveness measures
Employees whose records are too incomplete to assign them to a manager-effectiveness segment
Employees outside the HiPo population, if HIPO.1_bucket / HIPO.2_bucket indicate non-HiPo status

segmentation_strategy: Primary segmentation by manager-effectiveness terciles or quartiles using EL.1_sten, EL.2_sten, and EL.3_sten, with top-quartile vs bottom-quartile comparison as the focal contrast
Secondary segmentation by HiPo assessment level using HIPO.1_bucket and HIPO.2_bucket to examine whether predictive validity differs across assessment strata
Optional segmentation by manager coaching/feedback quality using 360.1_report through 360.8_report to test whether feedback quality strengthens or weakens the assessment-to-outcome relationship
Check consistency across the individual effectiveness dimensions (EL.1_sten, EL.2_sten, EL.3_sten) before using a composite or pre-registered index
potential_confounders: Baseline performance signals captured in 360.1_manager through 360.8_manager
Manager coaching and feedback quality from 360.1_report through 360.8_report, if not the focal moderator
Assessment score level itself when testing interactions among assessment bands
Role, job family, level, and tenure are important but are not available in the grounded columns; if absent, residual confounding should be acknowledged
Regression to the mean in performance ratings and any ceiling/floor effects in the rating scales
Manager span of control or business unit effects if they correlate with manager-effectiveness scores and advancement outcomes
filtering_rules: Restrict to records with non-missing values for the focal assessment score(s) and at least one manager-effectiveness measure
Limit to employees classified as HiPo using HIPO.1_bucket and/or HIPO.2_bucket, depending on the organization’s HiPo definition
If a single employee has multiple managers or repeated observations, define one pre-specified observation window and one manager assignment rule before analysis
Winsorize or flag extreme rating values only if pre-registered and consistent with data governance rules
Exclude rows where the manager-effectiveness index cannot be computed from the available EL columns without imputation rules that were not pre-specified
statistical_questions: Do HiPo assessment scores predict 12-month advancement-related proxies differently for employees reporting to top-quartile versus bottom-quartile managers on EL.1_sten, EL.2_sten, and EL.3_sten?
Is the assessment-score relationship with promotion-relevant performance ratings stronger under high manager effectiveness than under low manager effectiveness?
Does manager coaching/feedback quality (360.1_report through 360.8_report) explain additional variance beyond the main manager-effectiveness index?
Are the assessment scores systematically over-calibrated in low-effectiveness-manager settings, as shown by weaker correspondence between assessment and realized advancement proxies?
Do the interaction effects remain after controlling for baseline performance ratings and other available covariates?
business_questions: Which HiPo assessment scores are most useful for identifying employees whose potential is likely to translate into visible advancement under strong managers?
Where is the assessment system least calibrated: among HiPos reporting to low-effectiveness managers or in certain manager-feedback environments?
Should talent reviews incorporate manager effectiveness as a validation lens when interpreting HiPo results?
Can the organization improve promotion readiness signals by improving manager coaching and feedback behaviors?
Which manager effectiveness dimensions matter most for turning assessment promise into promotable evidence?
investigation_plan.validation_strategy: This hypothesis can be tested only partially with the grounded data. The available columns support a moderation-style validation of whether HiPo assessment scores are more strongly associated with manager-rated performance and related retention signals under higher manager effectiveness, and whether coaching/feedback quality adds explanatory power. However, the core outcome named in the hypothesis—promotion velocity—is ungrounded and has no proposed column proxy, so the promotion-specific claim cannot be directly validated from the provided schema. Likewise, stretch opportunity exposure and voluntary attrition are ungrounded. The analysis should therefore be framed as an indirect test of promotion-readiness calibration using available proxies, with a clear limitation that actual 12-month promotion velocity cannot be estimated without a dedicated promotion outcome field.
recommended_analysis_types: Interaction regression models with assessment score × manager-effectiveness terms
Stratified models comparing top-quartile vs bottom-quartile manager-effectiveness groups
Composite index construction for EL.1_sten, EL.2_sten, and EL.3_sten if pre-registered
Multilevel models if employees are nested within managers and manager-level clustering is material
Calibration plots comparing predicted assessment signals to observed manager-rated outcomes across manager-effectiveness strata
Sensitivity analyses using 360.1_report through 360.8_report as an alternate moderator
potential_risks: No direct promotion outcome is available, so the central hypothesis is only indirectly testable
Using performance ratings as a proxy for promotion velocity may not capture actual advancement decisions
Manager-effectiveness and manager-rating data may be endogenous if the same manager influences both ratings and outcomes
HiPo designation may already reflect manager input, creating selection bias
Potential missing data if only some managers or employees have 360 coverage
Overfitting if many personality and HiPo score fields are tested without a pre-specified composite or hierarchy
suggested_visualizations: Interaction plots of assessment score versus manager-rated outcome by manager-effectiveness quartile
Scatterplots with fitted lines for top- vs bottom-quartile managers
Coefficient plots for interaction terms across EL dimensions
Calibration curves comparing observed performance proxies across assessment-score bands
Heatmap of assessment score bands by manager-effectiveness bands against outcome means
Boxplots of performance ratings across manager-effectiveness quartiles

potential_mechanisms: High-effectiveness managers may convert HiPo assessment signals into visible advancement through better goal setting, advocacy, developmental assignments, and more accurate performance evaluation. In low-effectiveness settings, even strong HiPo scores may fail to translate into promotion-relevant behavior because coaching is weaker, feedback is less actionable, and opportunities are misallocated, creating an apparent over-calibration gap.

The negative association between HIPO.1_bucket and 360.1_manager suggests either a true contextual effect or a selection effect: higher-scoring employees may be disproportionately placed under weaker managers, or weaker managers may overrate/underrate differently. Either way, the manager context can distort the observed score-to-outcome mapping.

The 360.1_report through 360.8_report items may capture more proximal mechanisms than the aggregate manager index: specific feedback cadence, coaching quality, and developmental follow-through could explain incremental variance in advancement proxies above broad manager effectiveness.

If baseline performance is not fully controlled, the observed interaction between assessment score and manager effectiveness may partly reflect pre-existing performance differences, role difficulty, or unit-level talent sorting rather than a causal moderation effect.
supporting_evidence: simple_linear_regression on HIPO.1_bucket vs 360.1_manager shows a statistically significant negative slope (-0.0574, p=0.032678), indicating HiPo score is weakly associated with lower manager effectiveness context in this sample (R²=0.011, n=402).

descriptive_stats show HIPO.1_bucket is centered near 3 on a 1-5 scale with meaningful spread (mean=2.980, std=0.871, n=402), so moderation analyses are plausible because there is nontrivial variation in the predictor.

The user’s question itself is about differential prediction by top- vs bottom-quartile manager effectiveness and about adding 360.1_report through 360.8_report beyond the main index; however, the provided analytics results do not include interaction models, quartile splits, or incremental R², so these remain hypotheses rather than established findings from the current evidence.

The available evidence is consistent with a possible calibration issue: if higher HIPO.1_bucket values are not aligned with higher manager-effectiveness contexts, then simple score-outcome correspondence may be weaker in low-effectiveness settings due to contextual suppression or sorting.
alternative_explanations: Reverse causality or selection: stronger performers may be assigned to weaker managers, or weaker managers may be concentrated in certain units with different HiPo distributions, producing the observed HIPO.1_bucket–360.1_manager association without manager effects causing outcome differences.

Omitted-variable confounding: baseline performance ratings, job family, location, business unit, or span of control could drive both manager effectiveness ratings and advancement proxies, creating a spurious moderation pattern.

Measurement artifacts: 360-based manager effectiveness may partly reflect rater leniency, popularity, or employee satisfaction rather than actual managerial quality, which could distort the apparent relationship with advancement proxies.

Range restriction: if top-quartile and bottom-quartile manager groups differ in variance or sample composition, apparent interaction effects may be exaggerated or attenuated even when the underlying causal relationship is weaker.

The negative slope between HIPO.1_bucket and 360.1_manager is small (R²=0.011), so it may be statistically detectable yet practically minor; any downstream moderation could be noisy or unstable unless confirmed in fully controlled models.

narrative.executive_summary: The current evidence does not yet show that manager effectiveness changes the relationship between HiPo score and advancement. What we do see is a weak, negative association between HiPo score and manager-effectiveness context, but that pattern is not strong enough to support a conclusion about broken calibration or a true managerial moderation effect. Because the analysis did not include interaction tests, quartile splits, or incremental-fit comparisons, the main hypothesis remains untested rather than disproven. The more likely explanation is that sorting, confounding, or measurement breadth is obscuring the real mechanism.
storyline: Start with the core question: does manager effectiveness change how HiPo translates into advancement?
Explain that the required tests were not run, so the moderation claim remains unproven.
Note the observed weak negative association between HiPo and manager context, but caution against overreading it.
Shift from broad manager index to the possibility that specific 360 behaviors matter more than the aggregate score.
Close with the practical implication: separate true manager effects from sorting and confounding before changing talent strategy.
key_messages: No evidence currently supports or rejects HiPo × manager-effectiveness moderation; the test has not been executed.
The weak negative HiPo–manager-context relationship is not enough to infer broken calibration or a structural talent problem.
A broad manager index may be too coarse; specific behaviors like coaching cadence and developmental follow-through may be the real drivers.
Baseline performance, role difficulty, unit sorting, and rater bias could easily create a spurious pattern.
The next step is a controlled model with interaction testing and a search for the specific managerial behaviors that matter most.
narrative.business_narrative:
The central question here is whether manager effectiveness changes the way HiPo score translates into advancement. On the evidence provided, we cannot say that it does. The analysis does not include the key tests that would establish moderation—no interaction term, no quartile split comparison, and no incremental-fit assessment—so the hypothesis remains untested rather than unsupported. That distinction matters: the absence of proof is not proof of absence, and it would be premature to interpret the current results as a verdict on the quality of HiPo calibration.

What we can see is a weak negative association between HiPo score and the manager-effectiveness context in this sample. That pattern is directionally interesting, but it is too modest to carry a strong strategic conclusion on its own. It may indicate that stronger employees are being sorted into weaker managerial environments, or that manager ratings are being distorted by context, rater behavior, or other unobserved factors. It may also reflect measurement issues in the manager index itself. In other words, the pattern could be telling us something about where people are placed, or how managers are evaluated, rather than how manager effectiveness actually shapes advancement.

That is why the broad manager-effectiveness index should be treated cautiously. An aggregate score can be useful as a headline, but it may be too blunt to capture the proximate mechanisms that matter for promotion readiness. If advancement is really influenced by day-to-day coaching, feedback quality, follow-through on development plans, or the allocation of stretch assignments, then those behaviors are more likely to show up in specific 360 items than in an overall index. The practical implication is that the organization should not stop at asking whether “good managers” matter; it should ask which managerial behaviors matter, and under what conditions.

Before acting on any apparent mismatch between HiPo assessment and advancement outcomes, the organization needs to rule out the most likely confounders. Baseline performance, role difficulty, job family, location, business unit, span of control, and manager rater bias could all shape both the measured manager context and the advancement outcome. Without those controls, a pattern that looks like moderation could simply be selection or sorting. That is particularly important because the business risk cuts both ways: leadership could wrongly conclude that HiPo calibration is broken, when the deeper issue is talent placement or measurement noise; or it could miss a real manager effect if employees are systematically underdeveloped in weaker contexts.

The most defensible next step is a fully controlled model of advancement that includes the relevant background variables and a HiPo-by-manager-effectiveness interaction term. If that model shows a real effect, then the organization can move from diagnosis to intervention, focusing manager development on coaching, feedback quality, advocacy, and developmental assignment allocation. If it does not, then the problem is more likely to be in placement, measurement, or the definition of manager effectiveness itself. Either way, the analysis should move from broad labels to the specific mechanisms that convert potential into promotion.
supporting_evidence: No interaction, quartile-split, or incremental-fit results were provided, so the moderation hypothesis has not been directly tested.
HiPo score shows only a weak negative association with manager-effectiveness context.
There is sufficient spread in HiPo scores, so the lack of moderation evidence is not due to insufficient predictor variation.
A broad manager-effectiveness index may be too coarse to capture coaching cadence and developmental follow-through.
Observed calibration issues could be confounded by baseline performance, role difficulty, unit-level sorting, or manager rater bias.
contradictions: The data hint at a negative association between HiPo and manager context, but that is not strong enough to support a causal or structural interpretation.
The sample has enough variation to test moderation, yet the necessary tests were not run, so we cannot distinguish no effect from untested effect.
A poor-looking aggregate manager relationship may reflect true manager influence, but it may just as easily reflect sorting or bias.
The manager index may appear predictive at a high level even if the actionable behaviors are hiding inside individual 360 items.
future_questions: Does a HiPo × manager-effectiveness interaction predict advancement after controlling for performance and role context?
Do 360 items on coaching cadence, feedback quality, advocacy, and follow-through add incremental predictive power beyond the aggregate manager index?
Are high-potential employees disproportionately placed under weaker managers, in harder roles, or in specific units or locations?
Does the apparent negative HiPo–manager association persist after adjusting for baseline performance and span of control?
Are observed manager ratings themselves biased by unit, rater, or role context?
Which specific managerial behaviors most strongly predict advancement readiness and conversion from HiPo to promotion?

specs.title: HiPo score vs manager effectiveness: evidence for a weak association
specs.business_objective: Assess whether manager-effectiveness context is materially related to HiPo score before testing any moderation hypothesis.
specs.reason: The key reported result is a simple linear relationship between the two variables; a scatterplot is the clearest way to show direction, spread, and the very low explained variance. This directly supports the claim that the relationship is weak and negative.
specs.executive_message: HiPo score and manager effectiveness show only a very weak negative relationship, with substantial dispersion around the trend.
specs.expected_insight: Executives should see that the current sample does not support a strong structural link between HiPo score and manager-effectiveness context; any moderation hypothesis remains unproven.

specs.title: HiPo score distribution: enough spread to test moderation
specs.business_objective: Demonstrate that HiPo score varies sufficiently in the sample to make interaction testing feasible in principle.
specs.reason: The stated point is not association but range and dispersion. A histogram is the best way to show whether the predictor has enough variability for moderation analysis.
specs.executive_message: HiPo scores span the full 1–5 range with meaningful spread, so the lack of moderation evidence is not explained by a lack of predictor variation.
specs.expected_insight: The sample appears sufficiently varied to support interaction or subgroup analysis, meaning the absence of evidence is due to missing tests rather than a lack of data spread.

specs.title: HiPo-related profile across available 360 personality dimensions
specs.business_objective: Explore whether the more granular 360-report items show differentiated patterns that could proxy for coaching cadence or developmental follow-through better than a broad manager index.
specs.reason: The argument is that a coarse manager-effectiveness measure may be too blunt, and specific 360 items may be more informative. A multi-boxplot view can show whether HiPo scores align differently across item dimensions and whether any one item appears more discriminating.
specs.executive_message: If a broad manager index is masking the mechanism, the item-level 360 dimensions may reveal more actionable patterns than the aggregate measure.
specs.expected_insight: This view can indicate whether certain 360 item dimensions vary more systematically with HiPo score than others, suggesting where finer-grained measurement could improve interpretation.

specs.title: Potential calibration gap between two HiPo bucket measures
specs.business_objective: Check whether the two available HiPo bucket measures diverge materially, which could indicate calibration or measurement inconsistency worth investigating before deeper modeling.
specs.reason: Although the main question is moderation, the data also include two HiPo bucket variables with nearly identical summaries. A divergence chart is useful to quickly verify whether the two measures are aligned or whether there is systematic gap that could complicate interpretation.
specs.executive_message: The two HiPo bucket measures appear close on average; any meaningful separation would point to calibration or measurement issues rather than true signal.
specs.expected_insight: Executives can quickly assess whether the HiPo constructs are broadly consistent or whether there is a nontrivial divergence that merits governance or measurement review.

hypothesis_package.headline: The best predictor of performance depends on the role’s decision structure
hypothesis_package.summary: The hypothesis says different traits matter in different jobs: adaptability and learning agility should matter more in matrixed, cross-functional roles, while conscientiousness, persistence, and discipline should matter more in standardized, execution-heavy roles. If true, companies could improve performance and promotion forecasts by matching assessment signals to job design instead of relying o…
hypothesis_package.hypothesis_statement: Pre-specified psychometric signals will have differential predictive power by job architecture: in matrixed, cross-functional roles with high decision latency and dependency on influence, adaptability and learning agility will more strongly predict 12–24 month performance and promotion; in standardized, authority-led roles with low decision latency and high execution interdependence, conscientiousness, persistence, and task discipline will more strongly predict those same outcomes. The executive test is whether these trait-by-role interactions materially improve out-of-sample forecast accuracy for performance and promotion over a universal high-potential model.
hypothesis_package.mechanism_explanation: The mechanism is not generic person–context fit but variation in which capability converts into downstream business value under different decision environments. When roles require navigation across functions, ambiguity, and delayed information, traits that support rapid updating, social influence, and comfort with incomplete structure should translate into higher performance and faster advancement. When roles are defined by clear procedures, tight accountability, and repeatable execution, traits that support consistency, follow-through, and rule adherence should matter more. If the psychometrics are measuring real signal rather than assessment theater, these interactions should show up in pre-registered models and should separately move performance and promotion; if they do not, the evidence points to assessment invalidity or promotion-process noise rather than a true context effect.

dimension_notes.business_value: This would materially affect how executives validate and deploy talent models—potentially changing promotion, assessment, and development decisions by role family—though the value is tempered by limited downstream outcome data and the need to prove the fit effect before broad operational use.
dimension_notes.strategic_importance: This is strategically important because it directly affects succession planning, promotion decisions, and the credibility of the talent pipeline, with implications for leadership bench strength across role families; it is just below the very top tier because it is more of a talent-system optimization issue than a company-wide existential priority.
dimension_notes.actionability: If confirmed, the hypothesis directly supports concrete actions—building separate validation models by role family, tailoring selection criteria to decision context, and targeting development/placement to the environments where each profile is most predictive—making it highly operational rather than merely descriptive.
dimension_notes.organizational_impact: If validated, this would influence talent identification, role placement, and promotion decisions across multiple role families, affecting a meaningful slice of the leadership pipeline rather than a narrow process, though it likely stops short of changing core business strategy for the whole organization.
dimension_notes.feasibility: 4/16 target constructs map onto known data categories.
dimension_notes.novelty: The hypothesis is more nuanced than a standard “high potential predicts performance” claim because it introduces role-family-specific person–context fit and different trait weights by decision environment, but the underlying idea is still a familiar and widely studied HR/psychometrics theme rather than a highly original mechanism.
dimension_notes.depth: This hypothesis goes beyond a surface correlation by proposing a specific person–context fit mechanism—different traits predicting success depending on role ambiguity and authority structure—which is a genuinely deeper explanatory layer than simply saying some assessments work better than others.
dimension_notes.expected_insight: This hypothesis is likely to produce moderately high insight because it goes beyond a generic psychometric–performance link by positing a role-context interaction mechanism that could explain why the same traits predict advancement in some jobs but not others; however, the idea is still fairly plausible and familiar to talent practitioners, so the expected surprise is meaningful but not exceptional.
dimension_notes.confidence: The person–context fit mechanism is plausible and consistent with prior evidence on role contingency in talent prediction, but the current evidence base described is thin and vulnerable to confounding, so confidence is moderate rather than high.
dimension_notes.future_extensibility: This hypothesis is highly extensible because a confirmed person–context fit effect would naturally generate follow-on work on role-family-specific validation, segmentation of assessment models, development targeting, and broader tests across functions, geographies, and time horizons.

critique.executive_relevance: Moderate, but diluted by being a generic person–context fit claim. Executives may care in principle, yet this is not a sharp decision premise unless it is tied to concrete allocation choices (selection, promotion, rotation, or assessment design).
issues: The core idea is unsurprising: everyone already expects different roles to value different traits. That makes it weak as a hypothesis unless it identifies a specific, testable interaction with large effect size.
It is too broad. 'Role family' and 'decision context' are vague catch-alls. Without a pre-specified taxonomy, the analysis will be p-hacking in disguise.
It overlaps heavily with prior hypotheses about context-conditioned predictive validity and sponsorship/visibility effects. It adds little new mechanism beyond a dressed-up fit argument.

The mechanism is plausible but not yet discriminating. 'Person–context fit' explains almost anything after the fact; it does not specify why existing assessments should fail in one context and succeed in another.
It does not clearly distinguish whether the problem is assessment validity, job design, or promotion process noise. Executives need to know which lever to change.
The outcome bundle is muddy: 'later performance and promotion' are not the same outcome and may move differently. Mixing them weakens the test.
There is no clear threshold for what would falsify it. If some traits correlate in some roles, does that confirm the hypothesis or just show noisy heterogeneity?
The hypothesis risks tautology: if a role rewards adaptability, adaptable people do better. That is not a strategic insight unless it changes how the org selects, places, or develops talent.
It lacks a direct business consequence. 'Separate validation models by role context' is a process recommendation, not an executive decision with clear tradeoffs.
Downstream testing is feasible, but only if role families, outcome windows, and assessment constructs are frozen in advance; otherwise the analysis will be too flexible to trust.
suggested_improvements: Narrow the hypothesis to a specific contrast, e.g., 'assessment scores for adaptability will predict promotion in matrixed roles but not in standardized roles, while conscientiousness will show the opposite pattern.'
Define role families operationally using an existing job architecture or decision taxonomy, not broad labels like 'ambiguous' versus 'clear.'
Split outcomes: test performance, promotion, compensation progression, and tenure separately, rather than bundling them.

Specify the mechanism in a way that can fail, such as 'decision latency and cross-functional dependency increase the value of ambiguity tolerance, which should strengthen predictive validity in matrixed roles.'
State the executive decision implied: change assessment weighting by role family, alter placement rules, or retire specific constructs that do not generalize.
Pre-register the trait-by-role interactions to avoid retrospective storytelling and multiple-comparison inflation.

Consider adding a comparison to visible operational metrics, e.g., whether the same psychometric profile predicts manager-rated execution versus objective business outcomes, to determine whether the assessments are measuring signal or theater.
If the real question is pipeline quality, rewrite as a validation hypothesis about which constructs improve forecast accuracy for specific downstream decisions, not a generic fit statement.

grounded.rationale: This is the broad psychometric input space in the file: OPQ personality domains/facets, HiPo composites, leadership composites, motivation items, and 360 ratings are the available assessment signals.

These are the closest direct operationalizations of high-potential and leadership-related constructs; the HiPo buckets and enterprise leadership domain stens are explicit composites built for those purposes.
No column is named learning agility, so the best proxy is the broader OPQ/leadership potential signal, which may overlap conceptually but is not a direct measure.
Adaptability is not labeled directly, but the OPQ domain/facet structure under domain 3 is the most plausible trait-family proxy for flexible adjustment to changing demands.
There is no explicit ambiguity-tolerance column; the closest available proxy is broader leadership/potential composites, which may capture comfort with complexity only indirectly.

The leader-challenge fit percentiles are the best available operational proxy for influence in complex leadership contexts, though they are contextual fit indicators rather than a pure influence trait.
Persistence is not named directly, but OPQ domain/facet signals under domain 5 are the closest trait proxy for sustained follow-through and perseverance.
Execution discipline is best approximated by OPQ domain 6 and its facets/items, which are the strongest available proxies for structured, reliable execution behavior.
Manager feedback is directly represented by the manager-perspective 360 ratings; these are observer judgments rather than test scores.
Tenure is an explicit HR outcome column and would typically be used as a background control when modeling later performance or promotion.
Only current annual compensation is available; it can proxy pay level but not true compensation progression over time.

ungrounded.reason: No role-family or job-family column is available in the provided list.
No direct role-design or ambiguity column is available; the closest available signals are indirect fit or leadership composites, which are not the same construct.
No hierarchy/grade/level or span-of-control column is available in the provided list.
No columns proposed.
No columns proposed.

target_population.description: Employees with completed psychometric assessment data and usable manager feedback, observed over a 12–24 month outcome window for compensation progression and any available performance/promotion proxying within the provided data. Because no explicit role family, authority level, or promotion/performance outcome columns are available, this plan is limited to employees where the grounded assessment and compensation/feedback data can be linked and modeled, but it cannot directly test the stated job-architecture split without additional role-design data.
inclusion_criteria: Has at least one of the grounded psychometric assessment variables present
Has tenure_years recorded
Has compensation_annual_usd recorded
Has at least one manager feedback measure recorded (360.1_manager or 360.2_manager)
exclusion_criteria: Missing compensation_annual_usd
Missing tenure_years
No psychometric assessment data in the grounded columns
Records with obviously invalid or nonsensical numeric values after data quality checks

segmentation_strategy: Segment by available psychometric patterning rather than true job architecture, since role family, authority level, and role ambiguity are ungrounded and unavailable.

Create high vs. low cross-functional influence segments using the LC.*_pct block as an indirect proxy for influence-heavy work exposure, but treat this only as a coarse proxy, not a role-family substitute.
Compare higher vs. lower leadership/HiPo scores using EL.*_sten and HIPO buckets where present, as a secondary segmentation for differential signal tests.
If performance/promotion outcomes are unavailable, restrict segmentation to compensation progression bands and manager-feedback quartiles as the closest available dependent signal.
potential_confounders: tenure_years
compensation_annual_usd baseline level if a prior-period compensation measure exists in the analytic file
manager effects embedded in 360.1_manager and 360.2_manager
assessment completion timing relative to compensation observation window
selection bias from who received the psychometric assessment
organizational level differences not captured directly in the grounded columns
department or job-family differences if they exist outside the grounded list
filtering_rules: Exclude records with missing tenure_years or compensation_annual_usd.
Winsorize or otherwise screen extreme compensation_annual_usd values after confirming they are not legitimate outliers.
Keep only observations with assessment data collected before the outcome window, if timestamps are available outside the grounded columns.
If 360.1_manager and 360.2_manager are both present, predefine whether to average them or use the most recent; do not mix without a rule.
Do not attempt to infer role family, authority level, or promotion status from unavailable columns.
statistical_questions: Do psychometric scores and LC.*_pct measures predict compensation_annual_usd after controlling for tenure_years?
Do interaction terms between psychometric variables and the available cross-functional influence proxy improve out-of-sample prediction of compensation_annual_usd?
Do manager feedback variables add incremental predictive power beyond psychometrics alone?
Are the modeled effects stable across high vs. low LC proxy segments?
Is there evidence that the same psychometric factors have different slopes in different influence-exposure segments?
business_questions: Which psychometric signals are most associated with stronger compensation progression in the available data?
Do leadership/learning-agility-adjacent measures add value over a universal high-potential style model?
Can the organization improve forecasting by using interaction-aware models instead of a single universal score?
Are manager feedback and psychometrics redundant or complementary in predicting future pay progression?
What level of forecast lift, if any, is large enough to justify operationalizing a segmented model?
investigation_plan.validation_strategy: This plan can test whether grounded psychometric signals and indirect influence-exposure measures improve prediction of compensation progression and manager feedback, but it cannot fully validate the stated hypothesis because the key constructs role family, role ambiguity, authority level, performance acceleration, and promotion velocity are ungrounded and unavailable in the provided columns. As a result, any true job-architecture interaction claim must remain tentative: the available data support only an approximate test using LC.*_pct as a coarse proxy for cross-functional/influence-heavy environments and compensation_annual_usd plus manager feedback as outcome proxies. The proper validation step is to confirm whether additional job-design and promotion/performance columns exist outside the grounded list before interpreting results as evidence for context-specific trait utility rather than generic assessment signal.
recommended_analysis_types: Regularized regression with interaction terms
Out-of-sample cross-validation comparing universal vs. segmented models
Hierarchical/multilevel models if manager or team clustering is present in the full data
Incremental validity analysis / delta R-squared or delta AUC-style comparison depending on outcome scaling
Partial dependence or SHAP-style model interpretation for interaction exploration
Sensitivity analyses with alternative score constructions for EL and LC blocks
potential_risks: Key hypothesis constructs are missing, so the test is only an approximation.
Compensation is not a direct substitute for performance or promotion.
LC.*_pct may not faithfully represent role architecture or decision latency.
Manager feedback may reflect rater bias rather than true performance.
Psychometric scores may be highly correlated, creating multicollinearity.
Selection bias: only assessed employees can be studied.
Temporal misalignment between assessment and outcome windows may bias estimates.
suggested_visualizations: Interaction plots of psychometric score by LC proxy segment against compensation_annual_usd
Coefficient plot showing main effects and interaction terms with confidence intervals
Lift chart comparing universal vs. segmented prediction models
Calibration plot for out-of-sample compensation prediction
Heatmap of correlations among the psychometric and manager-feedback variables
Segmented boxplots of compensation_annual_usd by high/low psychometric tertiles and LC proxy bands

potential_mechanisms: The observed psychometric-to-compensation link appears very weak: the only directly tested example, 3_personality, has essentially zero explanatory power for compensation_annual_usd (R²=0.000). This suggests that either psychometric effects on pay are tiny, highly conditional, or overwhelmed by other determinants such as tenure, role, level, or manager-assigned pay bands.

A plausible mechanism is moderation by influence exposure: psychometric traits may matter more when employees have greater cross-functional interaction opportunities (higher LC.1.a_pct). In such settings, traits tied to social influence, coordination, or initiative could convert into visible performance or promotion signals that affect pay.

Another mechanism is segmentation: the same trait may have different slopes in high- vs. low-LC exposure groups because the job environment changes the extent to which personality is rewarded. In low-exposure roles, personality may be largely irrelevant to compensation; in high-exposure roles, it may be rewarded through stakeholder management or leadership visibility.

Manager feedback could add incremental predictive power if it captures performance perceptions that psychometrics do not. Compensation is often set using both objective tenure/grade rules and subjective managerial assessments; feedback may act as a mediator between observed behavior and pay, or as a more proximate signal than stable traits.

The bottleneck may be omitted variable bias or model misspecification: compensation is often driven by job family, band, geography, promotion history, and tenure. If these are not included, psychometric coefficients can wash out even when there are real causal effects, because the signal is absorbed by unmodeled structural factors.
supporting_evidence: simple_linear_regression on 3_personality and compensation_annual_usd found a near-zero relationship (R²=0.000, p=0.757661), which does not support a strong main effect of that psychometric variable on compensation.

descriptive_stats show the psychometric measures have reasonable spread (e.g., 3_personality std=1.484; 5_personality std=1.479; 6_personality std=1.359; 7_personality std=1.443; EL.1_sten/EL.2_sten/EL.3_sten around mean 3.5 with std ~0.75), so the lack of a strong simple relationship is not obviously due to zero variance.
LC.1.a_pct also has substantial dispersion (mean=0.682, std=0.267, min=0.018, max=1.000), which makes effect heterogeneity by exposure segment plausible to test.

Because only a simple bivariate regression is provided, there is no direct evidence here that tenure-adjusted effects, interaction terms, or manager-feedback increment improve prediction; the current evidence is mainly about weak univariate signal and the presence of enough variation to justify moderation analyses.
alternative_explanations: Reverse causality: higher compensation could be associated with job assignments or responsibilities that also influence psychometric test-taking context or measured responses, rather than traits causing pay.
Confounding by role level, job family, geography, or performance rating: these could drive both compensation and apparent psychometric differences, producing spurious or suppressed associations.
Range restriction and compensation compression: if most employees are in narrow pay bands or similar levels, psychometric differences may not map onto pay even if they affect promotion potential.
Measurement noise in psychometrics or compensation, or attenuation from imperfect proxies like LC.1.a_pct, could hide true effects.

The weak 3_personality result may not generalize to the other psychometric variables; without multivariable models, nonlinearity, or interactions, the available analysis cannot rule out more complex relationships.
Manager feedback may appear predictive simply because it is downstream of prior performance and compensation decisions, making it partly a consequence rather than an independent cause.

findings.statement: The only directly tested psychometric-to-pay relationship is effectively zero, so there is no current evidence of a meaningful main effect of personality on annual compensation.
evidence_refs: simple_linear_regression: 3_personality -> compensation_annual_usd (R²=0.000, p=0.757661)

findings.statement: Psychometric measures still have enough spread in the data that the null result is unlikely to be explained simply by a lack of variance in the traits themselves.

findings.statement: The current analysis is too limited to conclude that psychometrics do not matter for compensation; omitted structural drivers such as level, tenure, role family, geography, and promotion history could be absorbing the signal.
evidence_refs: context: only a simple bivariate regression is provided; supporting evidence notes no tenure-adjusted, interaction, or multivariable model

risks.description: Leaders may overinterpret weak bivariate evidence as proof that psychometrics are irrelevant, and then miss more conditional or indirect effects that matter in specific job contexts.
risks.affected_population: Employees in roles with high stakeholder exposure, leadership visibility, or promotion-driven pay progression

risks.description: If compensation decisions are being modeled without role, level, tenure, geography, and performance controls, the organization risks building misleading insights and poor pay policy decisions.
risks.affected_population: Workforce-wide, especially employees in heterogeneous job families and locations

risks.description: If managers are using subjective feedback in pay decisions without calibration, pay outcomes could reflect manager bias or inconsistent standards more than actual contribution.
risks.affected_population: Employees whose compensation is heavily influenced by manager assessments

opportunities.description: Test interaction effects between psychometrics and cross-functional exposure to identify whether certain traits are rewarded only in high-visibility, high-collaboration roles.
opportunities.potential_value: Could reveal targeted talent and reward levers for roles where influence and coordination drive business outcomes.

opportunities.description: Add manager feedback, tenure, level, job family, geography, and promotion history to compensation models to separate structural pay drivers from behavioral signals.
opportunities.potential_value: Improves pay-model accuracy and clarifies whether any psychometric signal is incremental or merely confounded.

opportunities.description: Segment the workforce into low- and high-exposure groups and compare slopes, rather than relying on a single average effect across all roles.
opportunities.potential_value: Can uncover where psychometric traits translate into compensation or advancement, enabling more precise talent management.

business_insights.financial_impact: No reliable financial estimate can be supported from the current evidence. At this stage, the likely impact ranges from negligible to material depending on whether omitted structural factors or interaction effects are uncovered in richer models.
business_insights.organizational_impact: The main organizational implication is that compensation appears to be driven much more by structural job and pay-setting variables than by personality alone. That means the business should avoid using psychometrics as a direct compensation input until incremental value is proven in a multivariable framework.
business_insights.employee_impact: Employees should not expect stable personality scores to influence pay in a uniform way across the organization. Any effect, if it exists, is likely concentrated in specific roles where visibility, influence, and managerial judgment matter more.

strategic_recommendations.action: Build a multivariable compensation model that includes level, tenure, job family, geography, promotion history, and manager feedback, then test whether psychometrics add incremental explanatory power.
strategic_recommendations.rationale: The current bivariate result is not enough to assess causality or practical relevance; this is the fastest way to distinguish true signal from confounding.
strategic_recommendations.expected_roi: High if it prevents incorrect policy decisions or identifies a real incremental driver; otherwise primarily diagnostic value.

strategic_recommendations.action: Test interaction terms between psychometrics and LC.1.a_pct, and compare low- versus high-exposure segments separately.
strategic_recommendations.rationale: The evidence and organizational logic both point to moderation: traits may only matter where cross-functional influence can convert them into visible performance or promotion signals.
strategic_recommendations.expected_roi: Potentially high in roles where collaboration and stakeholder management influence pay progression.
strategic_recommendations.owner_suggestion: People Analytics with HR Business Partners

strategic_recommendations.action: Validate manager feedback as a separate signal by checking whether it predicts compensation after structural controls, and whether it mediates psychometric effects.
strategic_recommendations.rationale: Feedback may be a more proximal indicator of performance than stable traits, but it may also simply reflect prior pay decisions; the distinction matters for policy design.
strategic_recommendations.expected_roi: Moderate; useful for understanding whether subjective assessments should be standardized or constrained.

strategic_recommendations.action: Do not use psychometric scores as a direct determinant of pay decisions until evidence shows stable, incremental predictive value.
strategic_recommendations.rationale: Current evidence shows no main effect in the only tested model, and using weak signals in compensation creates fairness and governance risk.

priority_ranking: Build a multivariable compensation model that includes level, tenure, job family, geography, promotion history, and manager feedback, then test whether psychometrics add incremental explanatory power.
Test interaction terms between psychometrics and LC.1.a_pct, and compare low- versus high-exposure segments separately.
Validate manager feedback as a separate signal by checking whether it predicts compensation after structural controls, and whether it mediates psychometric effects.
Do not use psychometric scores as a direct determinant of pay decisions until evidence shows stable, incremental predictive value.

narrative.executive_summary: The current analysis finds no meaningful direct relationship between psychometric traits and annual compensation, but that should not be read as proof that personality is irrelevant to pay. The null result is unlikely to be driven by too little variation in the psychometric measures, and there is a plausible case that any effect is conditional on work context, especially cross-functional exposure. At the same time, the analysis is too limited to separate behavioral signals from structural pay drivers such as level, tenure, role family, geography, and promotion history. The immediate priority is to build a multivariable model and test whether psychometrics matter only in specific roles or exposure contexts before drawing policy conclusions.
storyline: The only directly tested psychometric-to-pay relationship is essentially zero.
This does not appear to be a simple data-quality problem, because the psychometric measures still vary meaningfully across employees.
A more plausible explanation is that any signal is conditional, not average: traits may matter more in high-interaction or high-visibility roles.
The current model is too sparse to distinguish psychometric effects from structural pay drivers such as level, tenure, geography, job family, and promotion history.
The right next step is to test a fuller compensation model and then look for interaction effects and segment-level differences before using psychometrics in pay policy.
key_messages: There is no current evidence of a meaningful main effect of personality on annual compensation.
The null result is not easily explained by a lack of psychometric variation.
Cross-functional exposure is a credible moderator worth testing next.
Structural factors may be absorbing the signal, so the present model cannot support a broad conclusion that psychometrics do not matter.
Using psychometric scores directly in pay decisions now would be premature and creates governance and fairness risk.
narrative.business_narrative:
The headline finding is simple: in the only relationship tested directly, psychometric measures do not show a meaningful association with annual compensation. That is an important result, but it is not the same as proving that psychometrics are irrelevant to pay. In people analytics, a zero-looking bivariate result often means the signal is either weak, indirect, or buried under stronger structural drivers rather than absent altogether.

Here, the null result does not seem to be explained by a lack of variation in the psychometric measures themselves. The traits still have enough spread in the data that they could, in principle, differentiate employees. That makes the more likely explanation a modeling issue rather than a measurement issue: compensation is probably being shaped by factors not included in the current analysis, such as level, tenure, job family, geography, promotion history, and possibly performance or manager feedback. If those controls are missing, they can easily absorb the variance that might otherwise be attributed to psychometrics.

There is also a reasonable organizational case for moderation. Cross-functional exposure varies materially across employees, which means it is plausible that psychometric traits only translate into compensation when work requires visibility, collaboration, influence, or coordination across boundaries. In lower-interaction roles, the same traits may matter little; in high-exposure roles, they may matter more because they become visible through stakeholder management, leadership, or promotion signals. That means the right question is not simply whether personality predicts pay on average, but whether it predicts pay differently depending on the work context.

Because of that, the current analysis should be treated as a first pass, not a final verdict. It is strong enough to discourage any immediate use of psychometric scores as a direct input into pay decisions, but not strong enough to rule out conditional or indirect effects. The biggest practical risk is that leaders may overread a weak average relationship as proof that psychometrics never matter, when in fact the important effect may be concentrated in a subset of roles. The other risk is more serious: if compensation is being modeled without standard structural controls, the organization could reach confident but misleading conclusions and design pay policy around noise, bias, or incomplete comparisons.

The next step is straightforward. Build a multivariable compensation model that includes level, tenure, job family, geography, promotion history, and manager feedback, then test whether psychometric measures add incremental explanatory power. After that, examine interaction terms with LC.1.a_pct and compare low- versus high-exposure segments separately. If psychometrics only matter in high-interaction contexts, that is a meaningful business insight. If they still do not matter after robust controls, then the organization will have a much stronger basis for excluding them from pay decisions.
supporting_evidence: The only directly tested psychometric-to-pay relationship is effectively zero.
Psychometric measures still show enough spread in the data, so the null result is unlikely to be caused by insufficient trait variance.
LC.1.a_pct varies materially across employees, making cross-functional exposure a credible moderator to test.
The current analysis does not include key structural controls such as level, tenure, role family, geography, and promotion history.
Manager feedback has not yet been validated as a separate signal relative to structural pay drivers.
contradictions: The data show no average psychometric effect on compensation, yet the business logic suggests effects could still appear in specific contexts.
The psychometric measures are varied enough to test, but the current model is not rich enough to isolate their true contribution.
It is tempting to conclude that psychometrics do not matter for pay, but that conclusion is stronger than the evidence currently supports.
Manager feedback may capture real contribution, but it may also simply reflect prior pay decisions or subjective bias.
future_questions: Do psychometric traits predict compensation once level, tenure, job family, geography, promotion history, and performance controls are added?
Do psychometric effects emerge only in high-exposure or cross-functional roles?
Which specific traits, if any, are rewarded in high-visibility contexts versus routine roles?
Does manager feedback explain compensation independently, or is it largely a proxy for existing pay patterns?
Does manager feedback mediate any relationship between psychometrics and compensation?
Are there differences in slopes between low- and high-exposure segments that the pooled model hides?
If psychometric measures remain non-predictive after full controls, should they be excluded entirely from pay governance?

specs.title: Psychometric score vs. annual compensation: relationship is effectively flat
specs.business_objective: Test whether the directly observed psychometric trait explains annual compensation in the current sample.
specs.reason: A scatter is the clearest way to show the near-zero association and whether any pattern, outliers, or nonlinearity could mask a main effect.
specs.executive_message: There is no visible pay gradient by personality; the observed relationship is effectively zero.
specs.expected_insight: Points should appear broadly dispersed with no meaningful slope, reinforcing that the main effect of personality on pay is negligible in this dataset.

specs.title: Distribution of psychometric traits and cross-functional exposure
specs.business_objective: Show that the null pay result is unlikely to be driven simply by compressed psychometric variance or limited exposure variation.
specs.reason: Distribution charts are the best way to communicate that the psychometric measures and LC.1.a_pct have meaningful spread in the sample, supporting the interpretation that variance exists to detect effects if present.
specs.executive_message: The traits vary enough in the sample that the null result is not easily explained by a lack of variation.
specs.expected_insight: Psychometric scores should show broad coverage across their ranges, and LC.1.a_pct should display substantial dispersion, indicating sufficient heterogeneity for moderation tests.

specs.title: Cross-functional exposure may be the key moderator to test
specs.business_objective: Evaluate whether LC.1.a_pct is sufficiently dispersed to justify testing interaction effects with psychometric traits.
specs.reason: A focused distribution view on LC.1.a_pct makes the moderation argument concrete and avoids diluting the message across many variables.
specs.executive_message: Cross-functional exposure is materially heterogeneous, so moderation analysis is plausible and worth testing.
specs.expected_insight: The distribution should span low to high exposure, showing that some employees are in highly interactive roles while others are not.
